text: trees just off the road into town. Ronin had walked thousands of places like this. More than enough to know they weren’t always as empty as they appeared . Easing down on his belly in the brush, Ronin awaited nightfall . * * * A storm blew in with nightfall. Wind whipped through the deserted streets of Fort Collins, but the flashes of lightning were farther west, where dark clouds loomed over the mountains. There were lights in the hills — campfires, most likely — reminding Ronin that people did inhabit the area. Though the nearby rivers and lakes were far lower than they used to be, their water was an invaluable resource, and run-off from the mountains kept them fresh . He engaged night-vision as he entered town. The overgrown streets were empty, save for the husks of vehicles that had been stripped down to their frames long before. A surprising number of buildings were standing, though many had holes in walls and roofs. Waist-high stretches of wall and jutting steel supports were all that remained upright in some places . Ronin compared the place to its state during his last visit, 5,023 days before. Several more walls had collapsed, particularly on the brick structures, and the vegetation had spread, resulting in more damage . Rifle at the ready, he entered one of the buildings. He moved slowly, silently, and listened through the howling wind for any sound betraying the presence of another person. Though the search remained his primary function, his background processes turned toward Lara . Should he have taken her with him? He hadn’t lied about the danger, but at least he would’ve been near to defend her, if the need arose. If something happened in Cheyenne while Ronin was gone, she was all but helpless . He froze, a piece of rotting plywood forgotten in his hand. He’d left Lara vulnerable. How could he abandon her, knowing the history of that city? Knowing what had happened to the people who lived in those houses ? One of the journal entries surged up from his memory, words a shaky scrawl. There’d been a bloody fingerprint on the bottom right corner . I was going to head down to the bar again tonight. Don’t know why. The booze is sour and the company is bleak, but something’s been brewing there, and I ain’t talking about beer. People really worked themselves up these last few days, and I can’t blame them. I know it was stupid — hell, I knew it long before tonight — but I can’t blame them at all . I saw them from down 19 th , filling up the street. God, it was almost like those old movies, when all the villagers took up pitchforks and torches, only here it was shotguns and hunting rifles, crowbars and baseball bats. They were gathered up on the corner, shouting, and those bots…they just lined up across the street from the bar, shoulder to shoulder, and stood there. Didn’t move, didn’t say a goddamn word . Shit escalated. Shit fucking escalated . The bot that leads them stepped up. Told everyone to get their shit and go home. Someone threw a rock at it . Those things didn’t even bother wasting bullets. Didn’t need to. Most of them look human, but fuck…they tore through those people like humans are made of paper. Crowd fought for maybe thirty seconds, and then their nerve broke. Took off in all directions, a lot of them coming towards me. I helped Julio Ortega get around the corner. He wasn’t with his family when they got taken. When he let go of his stomach , his … God, his blood is all over me. I can’t. Can’t write this anymore . I don’t think they’re searching houses yet. Guess they’re content to slaughter people like cattle in the street, and that’s enough tonight. Like it was nothing … Ronin’s urge to return home was more powerful than ever, but it wouldn’t do any good; it was hours back to Cheyenne, even if he was quick, and the trip would’ve been wasted energy. He’d come all the way out here. He had to do what he’d intended . He searched the smaller buildings as the night progressed. Occasionally, their interiors lit up, followed several seconds later by wall-rumbling thunder. Bits of scrap metal and plastic accumulated in his rucksack. It would only be a small fraction of his last haul. The gold ring could up his return significantly, but it couldn’t be counted; it was Lara’s. Whether it should be traded was her choice . As dawn neared, the storm cleared out. Ronin moved into the larger structures near the town center. These showed more damage than many of the others — due, at least in part, to their size — but they were navigable . In the first, he discovered the remains of three humans — skeletons in ragged clothing. The fingers of the largest were wrapped around the grip of a rusted revolver. Ronin knelt, brushing away the dirt on the floor to reveal the message painted there . Saw their faces Heard their screams No one left to absolve us our sins He contemplated those words as minutes passed and the sun crept higher over the eastern horizon. Would they hold significance to Lara? Ronin could almost piece meaning together, could almost understand . Weren’t there images in his memory he couldn’t erase? Screams echoing from his otherwise forgotten past? Explosions and charging armies, gunshots and blood and oil … Ronin forced himself to stand and left the bodies behind. It would do him no good to pursue the fragments of another life . He went into the building with MNICIL — probably MUNICIPAL , before the Black Out — over the entrance. Sections of the ceiling had collapsed, likely due to decades of water damage, and most of the interior furnishings had been reduced to useless scraps. He
